content.text: Infrastructure as a Service sits at the foundation of the cloud service model stack. It is the layer that makes everything else possible — providing raw, programmable compute, storage, and networking resources that teams use to build everything from simple web servers to globally distributed, fault-tolerant platforms.
What It Is

Infrastructure as a Service (IaaS) is a cloud computing model in which a provider delivers fundamental computing resources — virtual machines, block storage, object storage, and networking — over the internet on a pay-per-use basis. The customer receives programmable access to these resources through an API and manages everything from the operating system layer upward: OS installation and patching, middleware, runtime environments, application code, and data.

IaaS is the most flexible of the three cloud service models (IaaS, PaaS, SaaS). It provides the building blocks without imposing constraints on what operating system, programming language, or software stack you use. AWS EC2, Amazon EBS, Amazon VPC, and Amazon S3 are the canonical IaaS services — together they supply the compute, persistent block storage, isolated network environment, and durable object storage that form the infrastructure layer of most AWS architectures.

The defining characteristic of IaaS is the shared responsibility split at the hypervisor boundary. The provider owns everything up to and including the physical hardware and virtualization layer; the customer owns everything from the guest operating system upward. This split gives the customer maximum control while outsourcing the operational burden of physical infrastructure.
Why It Matters

Before IaaS, deploying a new application required procuring physical servers — a process measured in weeks or months, with upfront capital cost regardless of whether the application succeeded. Organizations had to forecast their peak capacity years in advance and purchase accordingly, then operate that hardware through its full depreciation lifecycle even if requirements changed. Small teams and startups simply could not afford to compete with large enterprises that had the capital to build data centers.

IaaS solved these problems by converting capital expenditure into operational expenditure and making provisioning a software operation. A developer with an AWS account can launch a virtual machine in under 60 seconds, run it for an hour, and pay fractions of a cent. The same developer can launch 1,000 identical instances for a batch job, complete the job, and terminate all instances — paying only for the compute consumed. This is impossible with physical hardware at any price.

For AWS practitioners, IaaS is the layer they interact with most directly. Even when using higher-level services like RDS (managed databases) or EKS (managed Kubernetes), those services run on EC2 instances and EBS volumes underneath. Understanding IaaS deeply — instance types, storage performance characteristics, VPC networking, security group design — is prerequisite knowledge for working with any AWS service.
Benefits
Maximum flexibility.

No constraints on operating system, programming language, framework, or software stack. You can run any OS supported by the hypervisor (Linux, Windows, BSD), any runtime, and any application — including other hypervisors for nested virtualization.
Elastic capacity.

Instances can be launched or terminated in response to demand. Auto Scaling Groups automate this, adding instances when CPU rises and removing them when load drops — impossible to replicate with physical hardware without enormous idle capacity.
Global reach without capital cost.

Launch instances in any AWS region worldwide. Deploy a web server in Tokyo, a processing cluster in Frankfurt, and a backup in São Paulo — all through the same API, with no physical presence in those locations.
Programmable infrastructure.

Every IaaS resource — instances, volumes, network interfaces, security groups — is created, modified, and deleted through APIs. Infrastructure becomes code: versionable, testable, and reproducible across environments.
Hardware abstraction.

The hypervisor abstracts hardware details. AWS can replace failing physical servers, migrate VMs to new hardware, and upgrade network infrastructure without customer downtime. The customer's VM persists through hardware lifecycle events that would require manual intervention on-premises.
Diverse instance portfolio.

AWS offers 600+ instance types optimized for different workloads: general purpose, compute-optimized, memory-optimized, storage-optimized, GPU instances, and custom silicon (Graviton). Matching instance type to workload profile is a meaningful performance and cost lever.
Trade-offs
High operational responsibility.

With maximum flexibility comes maximum operational burden. OS patching, security hardening, middleware configuration, log management, backup strategy, and failure recovery are all your responsibility. PaaS and SaaS models outsource these tasks.
Complexity at scale.

Managing hundreds or thousands of EC2 instances requires orchestration tooling (Auto Scaling, Systems Manager, Ansible), monitoring at the OS and application layer, and careful capacity planning. The operational surface is proportional to the number of instances.
Slower application deployment cycle.

Launching an instance, waiting for it to boot, and deploying application code takes minutes. Serverless functions start in milliseconds; containers in seconds. For rapid horizontal scaling, IaaS is not the fastest model.
Cost complexity.

EC2 billing has many dimensions: instance-hours, EBS storage and I/O, data transfer, Elastic IP addresses, and more. Unused EBS volumes and stopped (not terminated) instances still incur storage charges. Cost visibility requires active monitoring.
Security requires active management.

AWS secures the physical layer, but guest OS vulnerabilities, unpatched packages, overly permissive security groups, and unencrypted volumes are the customer's responsibility. IaaS does not include managed patching — you must implement it explicitly with tools like AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager.
How It Works

When you launch an EC2 instance, the AWS control plane selects a physical host with sufficient capacity (CPU cores, RAM, local storage), instructs the Nitro Hypervisor to create a new virtual machine, attaches the specified AMI (Amazon Machine Image) as the root volume, configures the virtual network interface in your VPC subnet, and applies the specified security groups as a firewall. The entire process — from API call to instance in "running" state — typically takes 30–90 seconds.

Block storage (EBS) operates separately from the instance. An EBS volume is a network-attached block device that persists independently of any instance. When you attach an EBS volume to an instance, it appears as a standard block device inside the guest OS (e.g.,
/dev/nvme0n1
on Nitro instances). The data on the volume is stored on AWS's distributed storage infrastructure across multiple physical drives, with replication within the Availability Zone for durability.

Networking in IaaS is provided by the Virtual Private Cloud (VPC). A VPC is a logically isolated section of the AWS network where you launch resources. Within a VPC, you define subnets (IP address ranges), route tables (how traffic is directed), and internet gateways (connectivity to the public internet). Security groups act as instance-level firewalls, controlling inbound and outbound traffic per instance or group. Network Access Control Lists (NACLs) provide subnet-level filtering as an additional layer.

Object storage (S3) complements block storage with a different access model: instead of a file system, S3 exposes a flat namespace of objects (files) addressed by bucket name and key. S3 is not mounted as a block device — it is accessed via HTTP API (or the S3 SDK). This distinction is important: you cannot install an OS on S3, but you can store arbitrary data at virtually unlimited scale and access it from anywhere with internet connectivity.
Architecture: Responsibility Model and AWS Services
The diagram below shows the IaaS shared responsibility split and maps specific AWS services to each layer:

attrs.alt: IaaS shared responsibility model showing customer vs AWS managed layers with corresponding AWS services

content.text: Core AWS IaaS Services
Amazon EC2 (Elastic Compute Cloud).
Virtual machines. The primary compute primitive — launch an instance from an AMI, choose an instance type, and run any workload. EC2 is the foundation of most AWS architectures.
Amazon EBS (Elastic Block Store).

Persistent block storage volumes for EC2. Types include gp3 (general purpose SSD), io2 (high-performance provisioned IOPS SSD), st1 (throughput-optimized HDD), and sc1 (cold HDD). EBS volumes persist after instance termination and can be attached/detached from instances.
Amazon S3 (Simple Storage Service).
Object storage with 11-nines durability. Not a block device — accessed via API. Used for data storage, static website hosting, backups, data lake foundations, and AMI storage.
Amazon VPC (Virtual Private Cloud).
Isolated virtual network. Define IP ranges, subnets, route tables, gateways, and security rules. Every resource you launch lives inside a VPC.
Elastic Load Balancing (ELB).

Distributes traffic across multiple EC2 instances. Application Load Balancer (ALB) operates at layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS); Network Load Balancer (NLB) operates at layer 4 (TCP/UDP) with ultra-low latency; Gateway Load Balancer (GWLB) routes traffic through network appliances.
Amazon EC2 Auto Scaling.
Automatically adjusts the number of EC2 instances based on demand. Define scaling policies (target tracking, step scaling, scheduled scaling) and the group maintains your desired capacity.
When to Use IaaS
IaaS is the right model when you need control that PaaS or SaaS cannot provide:
Custom OS configuration or kernel modules required by the application
Migrating existing applications that run on specific OS versions or runtime environments (lift-and-shift)
Running workloads that require direct hardware access (GPU compute, HPC, FPGA instances)
Long-running processes that cannot be expressed as stateless functions (Lambda) or containerized applications (ECS/EKS)
Self-managed databases where the managed service (RDS, DynamoDB) does not fit the workload's requirements
Software licensing tied to CPU sockets or specific OS configurations (Oracle, IBM DB2)

IaaS is a weaker fit when the application is stateless and event-driven (Lambda is more cost-efficient), when the team lacks OS-level expertise and wants a managed platform, or when the workload is a standard application that a managed service (RDS, ElastiCache, OpenSearch) already handles correctly.
How to Implement: Core IaaS Workflow

The canonical IaaS workflow on AWS involves five steps: create a VPC and subnet, define security groups, launch an EC2 instance into that subnet, attach storage, and deploy your application. The following walkthrough uses the AWS CLI.
Step 1: Create the network foundation

# Create a VPC with a /16 CIDR block (65,536 addresses)
VPC_ID=$(aws ec2 create-vpc \
  --cidr-block 10.0.0.0/16 \
  --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=vpc,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=my-iaas-vpc}]' \
  --query 'Vpc.VpcId' --output text)
echo "VPC: $VPC_ID"

# Create a public subnet in us-east-1a
SUBNET_ID=$(aws ec2 create-subnet \
  --vpc-id $VPC_ID \
  --cidr-block 10.0.1.0/24 \
  --availability-zone us-east-1a \
  --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=subnet,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=public-1a}]' \
  --query 'Subnet.SubnetId' --output text)

# Create and attach an internet gateway for public internet access
IGW_ID=$(aws ec2 create-internet-gateway \
  --query 'InternetGateway.InternetGatewayId' --output text)
aws ec2 attach-internet-gateway --vpc-id $VPC_ID --internet-gateway-id $IGW_ID

# Create a route table and add a default route to the internet gateway
RTB_ID=$(aws ec2 create-route-table --vpc-id $VPC_ID \
  --query 'RouteTable.RouteTableId' --output text)
aws ec2 create-route --route-table-id $RTB_ID \
  --destination-cidr-block 0.0.0.0/0 --gateway-id $IGW_ID
aws ec2 associate-route-table --route-table-id $RTB_ID --subnet-id $SUBNET_ID
Step 2: Create a security group

# Create a security group (instance-level firewall)
SG_ID=$(aws ec2 create-security-group \
  --group-name web-server-sg \
  --description 'Web server security group' \
  --vpc-id $VPC_ID \
  --query 'GroupId' --output text)

# Allow HTTPS inbound from anywhere
aws ec2 authorize-security-group-ingress \
  --group-id $SG_ID \
  --protocol tcp --port 443 --cidr 0.0.0.0/0

# Allow SSH only from a specific IP (replace with your IP)
aws ec2 authorize-security-group-ingress \
  --group-id $SG_ID \
  --protocol tcp --port 22 --cidr 203.0.113.1/32

# All outbound traffic is allowed by default (do not restrict unless required)
Step 3: Launch an EC2 instance

# Get the latest Amazon Linux 2023 AMI
AMI_ID=$(aws ec2 describe-images \
  --owners amazon \
  --filters \
    'Name=name,Values=al2023-ami-*-x86_64' \
    'Name=state,Values=available' \
  --query 'sort_by(Images, &CreationDate)[-1].ImageId' \
  --output text)

# Launch a t3.micro instance (2 vCPU, 1 GB RAM, Nitro-based, free-tier eligible)
INSTANCE_ID=$(aws ec2 run-instances \
  --image-id $AMI_ID \
  --instance-type t3.micro \
  --key-name my-key-pair \
  --subnet-id $SUBNET_ID \
  --security-group-ids $SG_ID \
  --associate-public-ip-address \
  --iam-instance-profile Name=ec2-ssm-profile \
  --metadata-options 'HttpTokens=required,HttpEndpoint=enabled' \
  --block-device-mappings '[{"DeviceName":"/dev/xvda","Ebs":{"VolumeSize":20,"VolumeType":"gp3","Encrypted":true}}]' \
  --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=instance,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=web-server},{Key=Environment,Value=production}]' \
  --query 'Instances[0].InstanceId' --output text)

# Wait for the instance to enter running state
aws ec2 wait instance-running --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID

# Get the public IP address
aws ec2 describe-instances \
  --instance-ids $INSTANCE_ID \
  --query 'Reservations[0].Instances[0].PublicIpAddress' \
  --output text
Examples
Example 1: Attach and Mount an EBS Volume
EBS volumes are the primary persistent storage mechanism in IaaS. The following shows creating a volume, attaching it to an instance, and mounting it inside the guest OS:

# Create a 100 GB gp3 volume in the same AZ as the instance
VOLUME_ID=$(aws ec2 create-volume \
  --availability-zone us-east-1a \
  --size 100 \
  --volume-type gp3 \
  --iops 3000 \
  --throughput 125 \
  --encrypted \
  --tag-specifications 'ResourceType=volume,Tags=[{Key=Name,Value=data-volume}]' \
  --query 'VolumeId' --output text)

# Attach to the running instance
aws ec2 attach-volume \
  --volume-id $VOLUME_ID \
  --instance-id $INSTANCE_ID \
  --device /dev/sdf

# Wait for attachment to complete
aws ec2 wait volume-in-use --volume-ids $VOLUME_ID

# SSH into the instance and mount the volume
# (run these commands on the EC2 instance, not your local machine)

# Check what block devices are available
lsblk
# Nitro instances present attached EBS volumes as /dev/nvme1n1 (not /dev/sdf)

# Create a filesystem (only needed the first time)
sudo mkfs -t xfs /dev/nvme1n1

# Create a mount point and mount
sudo mkdir -p /data
sudo mount /dev/nvme1n1 /data

# Add to /etc/fstab for automatic remount on reboot
# Get the UUID first
UUID=$(sudo blkid -s UUID -o value /dev/nvme1n1)
echo "UUID=$UUID /data xfs defaults,nofail 0 2" | sudo tee -a /etc/fstab

# Verify
df -h /data
Example 2: Auto Scaling Group with Load Balancer
The most common IaaS production pattern combines EC2 instances with an Application Load Balancer and an Auto Scaling Group for elastic horizontal scaling:

# 1. Create a Launch Template (defines instance configuration) aws ec2 create-launch-template \ --launch-template-name web-server-lt \ --version-description 'v1' \ --launch-template-data '{ "ImageId": "'$AMI_ID'", "InstanceType": "t3.small", "IamInstanceProfile": {"Name": "ec2-ssm-profile"}, "SecurityGroupIds": ["'$SG_ID'"], "MetadataOptions": {"HttpTokens": "required"}, "UserData": "'$(echo '#!/bin/bash yum update -y yum install -y httpd systemctl start httpd systemctl enable httpd echo "

Hello from $(hostname)

" > /var/www/html/index.html' | base64)'" }' # 2. Create a target group for the load balancer TG_ARN=$(aws elbv2 create-target-group \ --name web-tg \ --protocol HTTP \ --port 80 \ --vpc-id $VPC_ID \ --health-check-path /index.html \ --query 'TargetGroups[0].TargetGroupArn' --output text) # 3. Create an Auto Scaling Group aws autoscaling create-auto-scaling-group \ --auto-scaling-group-name web-asg \ --launch-template LaunchTemplateName=web-server-lt,Version='$Latest' \ --min-size 2 \ --max-size 10 \ --desired-capacity 2 \ --vpc-zone-identifier "$SUBNET_ID" \ --target-group-arns $TG_ARN \ --health-check-type ELB # 4. Add a target tracking scaling policy (scale when CPU > 60%) aws autoscaling put-scaling-policy \ --auto-scaling-group-name web-asg \ --policy-name cpu-tracking \ --policy-type TargetTrackingScaling \ --target-tracking-configuration '{ "PredefinedMetricSpecification": { "PredefinedMetricType": "ASGAverageCPUUtilization" }, "TargetValue": 60.0 }'
Real-World Example

A financial services company migrated its risk calculation platform from on-premises to AWS IaaS. The platform runs end-of-day Monte Carlo simulations across a portfolio of 50,000 instruments, requiring roughly 2,000 CPU cores for 4 hours each evening. On-premises, this required permanently provisioning 500 physical servers (4 cores each) that sat idle during business hours.

On AWS, the platform uses an Auto Scaling Group with a minimum size of 2 (for the orchestration layer) that scales to 500 c6i.4xlarge instances (16 vCPUs, 32 GB RAM) nightly. Spot Instances cover 80% of the fleet at roughly 70% discount from On-Demand pricing, with On-Demand instances as fallback. Each evening, the ASG scales from 2 to 500 instances in about 8 minutes, the simulation runs for 4 hours, then the ASG scales back to 2. The on-demand compute cost is 4 hours × 500 instances × $0.68/hr (c6i.4xlarge) = $1,360/night — versus the annualized cost of 500 physical servers at $5,000 each = $2.5 million CapEx plus ongoing power and maintenance.

The platform stores simulation inputs on S3, uses EBS gp3 volumes for local working storage during computation, and writes results back to S3. AWS Batch manages job scheduling across the fleet. The entire infrastructure is defined in CloudFormation and can be redeployed to any AWS region within 15 minutes.
Common Mistakes
Storing data on instance store (ephemeral storage)
Some EC2 instance types (e.g., m5d, c5d, i3) include NVMe instance store disks — local SSDs physically attached to the host. These disks offer very high I/O throughput but are
ephemeral

: all data is lost when the instance stops, terminates, or is migrated to a different host. Using instance store for persistent data without copying it to EBS or S3 regularly leads to data loss when instances are replaced.

# ❌ Wrong: writing critical data only to instance store
# /dev/nvme0n1 is the instance store on c5d instances — data lost on stop/terminate
mkdir /data && mount /dev/nvme0n1 /data
echo 'critical data' > /data/important.txt

# ✅ Correct: use instance store as a scratch/cache disk,
# persist important data to EBS or S3
# Instance store for high-speed temp work:
mkdir /scratch && mount /dev/nvme0n1 /scratch
# EBS volume for persistent data:
mkdir /data && mount /dev/nvme1n1 /data  # EBS volume, survives instance stop

# Or sync to S3 periodically:
aws s3 sync /scratch/results/ s3://my-results-bucket/run-$(date +%Y%m%d)/
Using a single Availability Zone

Launching all EC2 instances in a single AZ creates a single point of failure. AZs are physically isolated data centers — an AZ outage (power, networking, or hardware failure) will take down all instances in that AZ simultaneously. For any production workload, run instances in at least two AZs behind a load balancer.

# ❌ Wrong: ASG limited to a single AZ
aws autoscaling create-auto-scaling-group \
  --vpc-zone-identifier "subnet-az1a"  # Only one AZ!

# ✅ Correct: span multiple AZs
aws autoscaling create-auto-scaling-group \
  --vpc-zone-identifier "subnet-az1a,subnet-az1b,subnet-az1c"  # Three AZs
# Auto Scaling will distribute instances across all three AZs
# An outage in one AZ leaves 2/3 of capacity running
Not terminating instances when done
Stopped EC2 instances do not incur compute charges, but they
do

still incur EBS volume charges (the root volume remains). Instances created for temporary tasks (experiments, one-off batch jobs, demonstrations) should be terminated, not stopped. Use lifecycle policies on development instances to enforce this automatically.
Security groups with 0.0.0.0/0 on sensitive ports
Opening port 22 (SSH), port 3389 (RDP), or database ports (3306 MySQL, 5432 PostgreSQL) to the world (

) is one of the most common IaaS security mistakes. Automated scanners on the internet try common credentials against any open SSH or RDP port within minutes of an instance launching. Use AWS Systems Manager Session Manager for SSH-less access, or restrict SSH to specific CIDR ranges.
Best Practices
Use IAM roles, not access keys, for EC2 instances.

Assign an instance profile (IAM role) to every EC2 instance. The instance can then call AWS APIs without hardcoded credentials. Rotating or revoking the role's permissions requires no change to the instance.
Enable IMDSv2 on all instances.
IMDSv2 (Instance Metadata Service v2) requires a session token to retrieve instance metadata, protecting against SSRF attacks that could steal IAM credentials. Enforce it with
--metadata-options HttpTokens=required
at launch.
Encrypt all EBS volumes.

Enable EBS encryption by default at the account level. Encrypted volumes at rest use AWS KMS keys and have negligible performance impact on Nitro instances. Unencrypted volumes may violate compliance requirements.
Use Spot Instances for fault-tolerant workloads.

Spot Instances offer up to 90% discount from On-Demand pricing. They can be interrupted with a 2-minute warning when AWS needs capacity back. Stateless, horizontally scaled workloads — web servers, batch processing, CI/CD agents — tolerate interruptions gracefully and benefit most from Spot pricing.
Automate OS patching with Systems Manager.

AWS Systems Manager Patch Manager applies OS patches on a schedule across your EC2 fleet. Define patch baselines (which patches are approved), maintenance windows (when patches apply), and patch groups (which instances get which baseline). This replaces manual SSH-and-patch workflows.
Define everything as infrastructure as code.

Use CloudFormation, CDK, or Terraform to define all VPCs, subnets, security groups, launch templates, and ASGs. Console-configured resources are not reproducible, not auditable, and not deployable to new environments or regions.
Performance and Scalability

EC2 performance is determined by three primary dimensions: compute (vCPU count, CPU type, clock speed), memory (RAM), and I/O (network bandwidth, EBS bandwidth). These are bundled in instance types — choosing the wrong type leaves the bottleneck resource underprovisioned while paying for unused capacity in others.

Network performance scales with instance size. Smaller instances (t3.micro, t3.small) have baseline network bandwidth of 5 Gbps with burst capability. Larger instances provide guaranteed bandwidth: c6i.4xlarge delivers 12.5 Gbps; c6i.8xlarge delivers 12.5 Gbps with two 25 Gbps ENIs; the largest instances support up to 100–400 Gbps. Placement Groups (cluster type) push instances onto the same physical rack, enabling the lowest inter-instance latency for tightly coupled HPC workloads.

EBS performance is configured independently of the instance type. gp3 volumes provide a baseline of 3,000 IOPS and 125 MB/s throughput, provisionable up to 16,000 IOPS and 1,000 MB/s. io2 Block Express volumes can be provisioned up to 256,000 IOPS per volume — designed for latency-sensitive databases like Oracle, SQL Server, and SAP HANA. Always set EBS-optimized mode (enabled by default on all current instance types) to prevent EBS I/O from competing with network traffic on shared bandwidth.
Tooling and Ecosystem
AWS CloudFormation / CDK.
Define VPCs, subnets, security groups, launch templates, and ASGs in code. CDK provides higher-level constructs (e.g.,
aws_ec2.Vpc
aws_autoscaling.AutoScalingGroup
) that generate CloudFormation.
AWS Systems Manager.

Operational platform for EC2 fleets. Session Manager provides browser-based SSH without opening port 22; Patch Manager automates OS patching; Run Command executes scripts across instances; Parameter Store manages secrets and configuration.
AWS EC2 Image Builder.
Automates AMI creation, testing, and distribution. Define a pipeline (base AMI → components → tests → AMI distribution) and Image Builder produces hardened, tested golden images on a schedule.
AWS Compute Optimizer.

Analyzes CloudWatch metrics from running EC2 instances and recommends right-sized instance types. Identifies over-provisioned instances (wasted cost) and under-provisioned instances (performance risk).
Terraform.
Provider-agnostic IaC tool with strong AWS support. Preferred for multi-cloud environments or teams with existing Terraform expertise. Maintains state files to track real resource state between runs.
Ansible / Chef / Puppet.

Configuration management tools that run inside instances to install packages, configure services, and maintain desired state. Ansible is agentless (uses SSH); Chef and Puppet use agents. Often combined with AWS Systems Manager for fleet-wide execution.
Comparison with Alternatives
Dimension
IaaS (EC2)
PaaS (Elastic Beanstalk)
FaaS (Lambda)
OS control
Full (you manage guest OS)
Limited (managed by platform)
None (fully abstracted)
Ops burden
High (patching, config, scaling)
Medium (deploy code; platform handles infra)
Low (deploy function only)
Startup latency
30–90 seconds (OS boot)
Minutes (deploy + health check)
< 1 second (cold start)
Pricing model
Per instance-second (min 60s)
Per underlying EC2 instance
Per invocation + GB-seconds
Best for
Custom OS, lift-and-shift, GPU, long-running
Web/API apps, standard runtimes, fast deploy
Event-driven, short-lived, unpredictable traffic
Security and Reliability
Security Group Design

Security groups are stateful virtual firewalls attached to network interfaces. "Stateful" means that if you allow an inbound TCP connection, the return traffic is automatically allowed — you do not need separate rules for outbound responses. Design security groups using the principle of least privilege: allow only the specific ports and protocols each tier requires, and prefer source security group references over CIDR ranges for inter-tier traffic.

# Preferred pattern: reference security groups instead of CIDRs for internal traffic
# This allows all instances in the "web-sg" group to reach the "app-sg" group on port 8080
# without hardcoding IP ranges that change as instances scale

# Create application tier security group
APP_SG_ID=$(aws ec2 create-security-group \
  --group-name app-tier-sg \
  --description 'Application tier' \
  --vpc-id $VPC_ID \
  --query 'GroupId' --output text)

# Allow inbound on port 8080 only from instances in the web security group
aws ec2 authorize-security-group-ingress \
  --group-id $APP_SG_ID \
  --protocol tcp \
  --port 8080 \
  --source-group $SG_ID  # web-server-sg reference, not a CIDR
High Availability Design

IaaS high availability on AWS is achieved through Availability Zone redundancy. The standard multi-tier pattern: an Application Load Balancer spans two or more AZs; EC2 instances are distributed across those AZs in an Auto Scaling Group; EBS volumes are replicated within their AZ (not across AZs — EBS is AZ-scoped). For cross-AZ data redundancy, use Amazon EFS (multi-AZ file system), Amazon RDS Multi-AZ (synchronous replication across AZs for databases), or S3 (replicated across AZs by default).
Mental Model

Think of IaaS as renting an empty plot of land with utilities (power, water, roads) already connected. The utility provider (AWS) maintains the infrastructure up to the property line — the physical data center, power supply, network connectivity, and the virtualization layer that defines your plot's boundaries. Everything on your plot — what you build, how you configure it, whether it has a fence, whether you patch the roof — is your responsibility.

PaaS, by contrast, is like renting a furnished apartment: the landlord (AWS) maintains the building structure, the plumbing, and even provides appliances and furniture (runtime, middleware). You bring your belongings (application code and data) and configure the space, but you cannot knock down walls or replace the plumbing. Serverless is like staying in a hotel: you use the room when you need it, leave when you don't, pay only for nights stayed, and have zero control or responsibility for anything beyond your personal items.

The model choice maps directly to the control-vs-responsibility trade-off. More control requires more expertise and operational investment. Less control means faster time-to-value but reduced flexibility. IaaS maximizes control; serverless minimizes operational burden. Most real AWS architectures combine all three — IaaS for workloads needing OS-level control, PaaS for standard web services, and serverless for event-driven functions — choosing the right model for each component.
Key Takeaways

IaaS provides raw compute (EC2), storage (EBS, S3), and networking (VPC) on a pay-per-use basis. The provider manages physical infrastructure and the hypervisor; the customer manages everything from the guest OS upward.

Maximum flexibility is the defining IaaS advantage — any OS, any runtime, any software stack, any configuration. This comes at the cost of maximum operational responsibility: OS patching, security hardening, scaling, and failure recovery are all customer tasks.

EC2 Auto Scaling Groups combined with Application Load Balancers across multiple Availability Zones is the standard IaaS high-availability pattern. Single-AZ deployments are a single point of failure.

EBS volumes are the persistent block storage primitive. Attach one to an EC2 instance, format it, and use it like a disk — it persists independently of the instance. Instance store (local NVMe on some instances) is ephemeral and loses data on stop/terminate.

Security groups are the primary network firewall. Use the principle of least privilege, reference security group IDs instead of CIDRs for inter-tier traffic, and never open administrative ports (22, 3389) to 0.0.0.0/0.

Spot Instances provide up to 90% cost savings for fault-tolerant workloads. Use them for stateless web servers, batch processing, CI/CD workers, and data processing pipelines — any workload that can handle a 2-minute interruption notice.

IaaS is the right model when you need OS-level control, are migrating existing applications, or run workloads (GPU, HPC, licensed software tied to hardware) that higher-level services cannot accommodate.
